ABEL, SIR FREDERICK Auousrus, BART. 1846. Student, Royal

Born 1827. Studied Chemistry under Professor Hofmann, 1846. Assist
ant at the Royal College of Chemistry, 1847-51. 1854, Chemical Referee
to the Government. I855, Chemist to the War Department. I867,
Appointed Member of the Ordnance Committee. 1883, Member of the
Royal Commission on Accidents in Mines. Organising Secretary and
Director of the Imperial Institute. President of the Government Com
mittee on Explosives.
Sir Frederick Abel was created C.B. in 1877; K.C.B. in 1893; Baronet
in 1894. He was President of the British Association at Leeds in 1890.
He has been President of the Chemical Society, the Iron and Steel
Institute, the Institute of Chemistry, and of the Society of Chemical
Industry; also Chairman of the Society of Arts. He is a Fellow of the
Royal Society, and has been Bakerian Lecturer, Rede Lecturer, Recipient
of the Royal Medal, the Telford Medal, and the Albert Medal. He is
author of The Modern History of Gunpowder; Gun Cotton; Explosive
Agents, and other works connected with Explosives and Chemistry.

1893, on the retirement of Mr. Edward Best, he was appointed to the
entire charge of this ofiice. For some years he took a leading part in
the publication of the Geological Record, undertaking the post of Editor in
1887. For many years he was on the Council of the London Geological
Society, and also President and Vice-President of the Geologistsĺ Associa
tion. He was the author of over eighty published memoirs and papers,
and of more than twenty sheets of geological maps. He wrote a Report
on the Geological Distribution of Gold and Silver ; investigated the
Occurrence of Phosphates, and wrote several papers on the subject of
ô Coal in Kent.ö From I875 he was Examiner in Geology to the Durham
University, and at the Newcastle College of Science. He also succeeded
Mr. Bristow as Examiner in Geology to the Science and Art Department.
He died at Croydon in September 1894.

Military Service.ŚOct. 1914 Joined the 12th Artirsky Hussars. Was in
the victorious march over the Carpathians into Hungary, and was
wounded ; June 1915 Galitz. Decorated with all the four Crosses of St.
George. The Times correspondent wrote in January 1916 when visiting the
Bukowina, that the Cavalry Brigade had accorded him an ovation in
memory of the Englishman who had served with them, and who, they
informed him with pride, had won all four Crosses of St. George, and
twice been recommended as an officer, an unprecedented honour for a
foreigner, for which a military law had to be altered. In December
1914 the first Cross was won for a midnight reconnaisance to the enemy's
lines. The second Cross in March 1915 when with 8 men, all of whom
received medals, an Austrian column was attacked and 88 prisoners
taken. The third was given for carrying cases of ammunition to the
firing line, after orders had been given to abandon an impossible task.
The record of the deed which won the fourth Cross has so far not reached
England.
